1.synthesis: Paul grounds the entire ethical appeal on the resurrection reality: 'If then you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God' (v.1). Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth (v.2). The reason: 'For you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory' (vv.3–4). The imperative of seeking heavenly things rests on the indicative of dying and rising with Christ; the hiddenness of the present life anticipates the revelation of the future glory.
1.historical_context: Gill notes Paul 'does not here call in question their being risen with Christ, but takes it for granted that they were.' The 'if' is the first-class condition that assumes the truth of the protasis — since you have been raised with Christ. The resurrection with Christ was enacted at conversion, when faith united them to the risen Lord; the ethical call is to bring daily life into conformity with the already-established ontological reality. 'Seated at the right hand of God' (v.1) cites Psalm 110:1, the royal enthronement text that the early church used consistently to describe Christ's exaltation — grounding the ethical imperative in the present cosmic reality of Christ's sovereignty.
1.literary_structure: The four verses function as the hermeneutical key for all of chapters 3–4. The pattern is: resurrection reality (v.1a) → imperative to seek above (v.1b) → imperative to set mind on above (v.2) → grounds in present hiddenness (v.3) → grounds in future revelation (v.4). The present-hiddenness and future-revelation structure anticipates two of the major theological moves of the chapter: the present battle against the flesh (vv.5–17) and the future appearance with Christ in glory. Hidden life is not dormant life but secretly powerful life — like a seed underground, it is alive and growing toward a revelation that will astonish.
1.theological_application: Gill on 'set your affections on things above' (v.2) unpacks the verb's full scope: 'to mind them, and think on them, to favour and approve of them, to be affectionately desirous of them, and concerned for them; for where the treasure is, the heart should be; and as the saints' best things are above, their minds and affections should be there likewise; their contemplation should be on those things, and their conversation should be in heaven.' This is not a call to other-worldly disengagement from present duties but to a reorientation of the affective center — the mind and heart drawn toward Christ as the organizing center of all value. The contrast with the errorists' earthly-minded ascetic system is pointed: the errorists' regulations were earthly things in Paul's sense — fixated on bodily practices and earthly religious performances — rather than on Christ above.
1.christology: 'When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory' (v.4) names Christ as 'your life' — not merely the source or the model of life, but life itself. This is the Johannine claim in Pauline idiom: 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25); 'I came that they may have life' (John 10:10). The believers' life is currently hidden because Christ himself is hidden — ascended, not yet visibly reigning. When he appears at the parousia, the hiddenness is dissolved: 'you also will appear with him in glory' — the appearing of Christ and the appearing of his people are a single event, the same glory revealed in and through him reaching them because they are in him.

5.synthesis: From the resurrection indicative Paul moves to the mortification imperative: 'Put to death therefore what is earthly in you: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry' (v.5). On account of these, the wrath of God is coming; and the Colossians themselves once walked in these things (vv.6–7). But now they must put them all away: anger, wrath, malice, slander, obscene talk, and lying to one another — 'seeing that you have put off the old self with its practices and have put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator' (vv.9–10). In this renewal there is no Greek or Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian or Scythian, slave or free — Christ is all, and in all (v.11).
5.historical_context: Chrysostom uses vivid pastoral rhetoric to press the mortification command: 'I know that many are offended by the foregoing discourse, but what can I do? ye heard what the Master enjoined. Mortify, he saith, your members which are upon the earth; fornication, uncleanness, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry. What is worse than such a covetousness?' The identification of covetousness as idolatry (v.5) is shared with Ephesians 5:5 and reflects the prophetic tradition that identifies desire-for-things as the practical substitution of the thing desired in place of God. The social catalogue of v.11 — Greek/Jew, circumcised/uncircumcised, barbarian/Scythian, slave/free — maps the full range of first-century social categorizations and declares them abolished in Christ.
5.literary_structure: The section follows the put-off/put-on structure standard in Pauline ethical exhortation (cf. Ephesians 4:22–24). The put-off list (vv.5–9) addresses sins first of the body/desire (v.5) then of speech (v.8), grounded in the already-accomplished theological reality of having 'put off the old self' (v.9). The put-on (vv.10–11) is not a list of virtues but a person: the new self 'renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator' is being renewed toward the divine image that Adam marred. The climactic 'Christ is all, and in all' (v.11) is the social and ontological conclusion: in the new-creation community shaped by Christ's renewal, the social divisions that structured ancient identity are overridden by the one identity — being in Christ.
5.theological_application: The mortification command 'put to death' (nekrōsate) is a strong aorist imperative — a decisive, once-for-all act demanded, not merely an ongoing process. But it is preceded by the indicative 'you have died' (3:3) — they are to accomplish decisively in practice what is already true in Christ. Gill on the new self 'renewed in knowledge' (v.10) identifies this as the restoration of the image lost in the fall: 'before man fell he was endowed with righteousness, true holiness, and knowledge' — the renewal is a re-creation toward the original human vocation of imaging God in knowledge, righteousness, and holiness. This renewal is 'after the image of its creator' — the divine image being restored is God himself, and the image par excellence is Christ, so renewal in knowledge is renewal toward Christ-likeness.
5.christology: 'Christ is all, and in all' (v.11) is the letter's most compressed christological statement and its most radical social claim. Christ is 'all' — the totality of what matters, the one in whom all distinctions of status, ethnicity, and condition are overridden. 'In all' — present within every member of the new humanity regardless of their social location. The renewal 'after the image of its creator' leads here: as Adam was renewed toward the divine image, so the new humanity is being renewed toward Christ, and this renewal produces the community where he is literally all that counts. The errorists' system preserved and reinforced social distinctions through its graduated access to spiritual privilege; Paul's Christ collapses every hierarchy.

12.synthesis: As God's chosen ones, holy and beloved, the Colossians are to put on heartfelt compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience — bearing with one another and forgiving each other, as the Lord has forgiven them (vv.12–13). And above all, love — which is the bond of perfectness (v.14). Let the peace of Christ rule in their hearts, to which they were called in one body, and let them be thankful (v.15). Let the word of Christ dwell in them richly — teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs with thankfulness in their hearts to God (v.16). And whatever they do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him (v.17).
12.historical_context: Chrysostom on 'let the word of Christ dwell in you richly' (v.16) connects the command to the practice of communal Scripture use: 'Let the word of Christ dwell in you'; that is, the teaching, the doctrines, the exhortation, wherein He says, Come unto Me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. The psalmody instruction (psalms, hymns, spiritual songs) reflects the early church's practice of corporate sung worship as a vehicle for doctrinal formation — a direct counter to the errorists' attempts to shape the community through their own teaching. The repeated thanksgiving theme throughout vv.15–17 connects to the letter's running critique of the errorist system as ungrateful — seeking what has already been given.
12.literary_structure: The put-on list builds cumulatively: individual virtues (v.12) → interpersonal practices (v.13) → love as crown (v.14) → Christ's peace as arbitrator (v.15) → Christ's word as indweller and teacher (v.16) → Christ's name as the frame of all action (v.17). The movement is from inner character through relational practice to communal worship to total-life orientation. Love is placed 'above all' not as a replacement for the other virtues but as their binding: Gill identifies love as 'that which will unite all other things, and make them complete. Without love, though there might be other graces and virtues, they would be like loose, unconnected links of a chain; but love binds them all together in one beautiful, harmonious whole.'
12.theological_application: Gill on the put-on virtues traces them to their christological ground: the Colossians are to put them on 'as the elect of God, holy and beloved' — which is not merely an ethical credential but a theological identity marker. They put on these graces not to become beloved but because they already are. Gill reads 'heartfelt compassion' (splanchna oiktirmou) as 'tender mercy and compassion which affect the bowels, or the most tender part of the soul; that springs from a principle of love, and is expressed towards persons in any distress, or who labour under any infirmity of body or mind.' The theological application of forgiveness ('as the Lord has forgiven you,' v.13) is the most searching: the standard and ground of mutual forgiveness is Christ's own forgiveness of them — not proportionate to offense received but modeled on grace given without desert.
12.christology: The triple occurrence of Christ/Lord in vv.15–17 — the peace of Christ (v.15), the word of Christ (v.16), the name of the Lord Jesus (v.17) — establishes Christ as the center and circumference of the community's life. The 'peace of Christ' that rules in the heart is the eschatological shalom that Christ himself made through his cross (1:20), now functioning as the internal arbitrator of community decisions. The 'word of Christ' that dwells richly is not merely teaching about Christ but Christ's own authoritative word, working actively in the community as a living presence. And 'the name of the Lord Jesus' that frames every word and deed is the invocation of his lordship over every domain — the practical answer to the errorists' claim that other names and powers must be propitiated.

18.synthesis: Paul turns to the household code: wives submit to husbands, as is fitting in the Lord (v.18); husbands love your wives and do not be harsh with them (v.19); children obey your parents in everything, for this pleases the Lord (v.20); fathers do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged (v.21); servants obey your earthly masters in everything, not by way of eye-service as people-pleasers, but with sincerity of heart, fearing the Lord — for it is the Lord Christ whom you serve (vv.22–24). The wrongdoer will be repaid for what he does, and there is no partiality (v.25).
18.historical_context: Chrysostom observes the strategic placement of the household code: 'Why does he not give these commands everywhere, and in all the Epistles, but only here, and in that to the Ephesians, and that to Timothy, and that to Titus? Because probably there were dissensions in these cities.' The household code addresses the immediate social context where the errorist teaching was likely producing social disruption, particularly through its ascetic and separatist tendencies. Gill notes that servants are to obey 'not to the conscience, and religious worship; in things worldly, and not spiritual; in all things that are within a master's power, and it is lawful for him to command.' The qualifier 'in the Lord' and 'fearing the Lord' that frames each relationship is not merely a pious overlay but a theological transformation of each relationship's ground and purpose.
18.literary_structure: The code addresses three pairs of relationships in descending social order: wives-husbands, children-fathers, servants-masters. Each relationship receives both the subordinate's duty and the superior's counter-duty, preventing the code from being read as mere legitimization of hierarchy. The extended application to servants (vv.22–25) may reflect the demographic reality that a significant portion of the early church's membership were slaves. The theological rationale builds through the passage: 'fitting in the Lord' (v.18), 'pleases the Lord' (v.20), 'fearing the Lord' (v.22), 'as to the Lord' (v.23), 'you are serving the Lord Christ' (v.24). Every relationship is reoriented by the same lordship.
18.theological_application: Gill on the servants passage draws out the transformation of motive: obedience 'not with eyeservice, as menpleasers' means not performing only when observed or when performance brings rewards from the human master; 'but in singleness of heart, fearing the Lord' means an undivided, integrated disposition oriented toward God. The key theological insight is that the servants' real master is 'the Lord Christ' (v.24) — a radical reversal of the ancient world's assumption that slaves had no standing before God distinct from their masters. The threat of v.25 — 'the wrongdoer will be repaid for the wrong he has done, and there is no partiality' — addresses masters as much as servants: God does not favor the free over the enslaved in his judgment.
18.christology: The repeated anchoring of household duties to 'the Lord' or 'Lord Christ' throughout vv.18–24 is the christological transformation of the ancient household code. Every relationship now has a third party: the Lord Jesus, whose lordship relativizes every other lordship (husband over wife, parent over child, master over slave) without abolishing it. The servant's 'you are serving the Lord Christ' (v.24) is remarkable: the lowest member of the social hierarchy is described as serving the cosmic Lord — a dignity that no social system could confer. Christ's lordship is the ultimate democratization of dignity: all serve the same Lord, and the Lord sees and rewards all without partiality.
